text: In February, my supervisor handed me an unexpected assignment: to restore a declining manor in rural Europe, a project that would involve months of careful work. The architecture was modern in some wings and aboriginal in others, a complex blend that no alphabetical guidebook could fully paraphrase. A weathered signpost by the sidewalk pointed the way, and a cab would haul visitors and their luggage, each suitcase heavy with hope, up the long drive. The supervisor's reputation was built on aesthetic judgment; he would praise good craft and notify the owners of any flaw, however small. I would cram my notes each night, look at faded photographs, and feel a wave of nostalgia for a bygone age of aristocracy and ceremonial pomp. The damp had caused corrosion in the cable and a slow trickle of rust down the precious stone. By February's end, after a deluge of rain, the cellar nearly became a pond, yet the source of the leak proved easy to find. Atmospheric moisture would pervade every room, and a single papercut on my finger, caustic with dust, reminded me how painful old buildings can be. A rheumatic gardener, reliant on a cane, would yield his secrets slowly: which soil was fertile, where irrigation would nourish the roses, and how a snail or a spider might lurk under a windowsill. He would eat lunch on the lawn and watch a chick wander by, his eloquence quiet but real. The manor's revenue had fallen badly; a subscription scheme and a modest tariff on tours were our attempt to defray costs without lax accounting. One quirk of the estate was a sealed vault rumored to hold a knight's skull and an excerpt of an old treaty once tainted by treason. We could possibly open it tomorrow, but I felt a nerve of dread. Terrestrial maps, an imaginary line of an Antarctic expedition, an endorsement from a famous historian, all came to fruition as the work advanced. The kernel of the plan, its operative idea, was to outweigh decay with care. Demography studies showed the village was cool to tourism, so we would practise restraint. An impromptu party would terrify no one; a chaste, double-arched hall, a precious eclipse of evening light through the glass, the gentle transport of memory anywhere I walked, all proved that a productivity born of patience could revive even the most bygone of homes. I found an old wallet on a windowsill, a precious resource of faded photographs, and I would suffer no haste in restoring them. Every extra hour would become a gift, and the village would become whole again. The work would not be done in a single month, but it would be done.
translation: 二月里，我的主管交给我一项意外的任务：修复欧洲乡间一座日渐衰败的庄园，这个项目将牵涉数月的细致工作。它的建筑在某些侧翼是现代的，在另一些则带有原住民风格，是一种复杂的混合，任何按字母排列的指南都无法完全转述。人行道旁一块饱经风霜的路标指明了方向，一辆出租车会把访客和他们的行李——每只手提箱都装满希望——拉上长长的车道。主管的声誉建立在审美判断之上；他会赞美好的手艺，并把任何瑕疵，无论多小，都通知业主。每晚我会临时苦读我的笔记，看着褪色的照片，对一个贵族与典礼盛况的逝去时代涌起一阵怀旧。潮湿使电缆腐蚀，让锈迹沿着珍贵的石头缓缓流下。到二月末，一场倾盆大雨之后，地窖几乎成了池塘，但渗漏的源头很容易找到。空气中的湿气会弥漫每一个房间，我手指上一道沾着尘土、刺痛的纸割伤，提醒我老建筑会有多么痛苦。一位患风湿、依赖手杖的园丁会缓缓吐露他的秘密：哪块土壤肥沃，哪里的灌溉能滋养玫瑰，以及一只蜗牛或一只蜘蛛会如何潜伏在窗台之下。他会在草坪上吃午饭，看着一只小鸡踱步而过，他的口才安静却真实。庄园的收入下滑得很厉害；一项订阅方案和一笔对参观团征收的适度关税，是我们在不放松账目的前提下分摊成本的尝试。庄园的一个怪处是一间封闭的地下室，传闻藏着一位骑士的头骨和一份曾被叛国玷污的旧条约的摘录。我们或许明天就能打开它，但我感到一阵恐惧的神经悸动。陆地地图、一条南极探险的假想路线、一位著名历史学家的背书，都随着工作的推进开花结果。计划的核心，它起作用的理念，是用悉心照料去抵过腐朽。人口学研究表明这个村庄对旅游业态度冷淡，所以我们会克制行事。一场即兴的聚会不会吓到任何人；一座纯净的双拱大厅、傍晚光线透过玻璃那珍贵的食般遮蔽、记忆在我走到任何地方时的温柔传送，都证明了一种由耐心而生的生产力，能让即便最久远的家园也焕发生机。我在一扇窗台上发现了一只旧钱包，里面是一份珍贵的、装着褪色照片的资源，我修复它们时绝不容许有半点仓促。每一个额外的小时都会成为一份礼物，而这座村庄也将重新变得完整。这工作无法在一个月内完成，但它终将完成。
